comments.text: I'm having trouble reconstructing the de Bruijn-Erdos-Turan claim $\sum_{n < x} f(n)^2 \sim x$ (which is not detailed in the reference given). Upper bound sieves can give a bound of the shape $\sum_{n < x} f(n)^2 \ll x$, but to get the asymptotic seems to require an understanding of the error term in the prime number theorem in almost all intervals of the form $[x, x+x^\theta]$ for $\theta$ close to zero, and at our current level of understanding this is only available on the density hypothesis (and hence on the Lindelof or Riemann hypothesis). In another direction, it seems likely that one can at least show that the lim sup will be infinite assuming the Hardy-Littlewood prime tuples conjecture, by working with suitable dense clusters of primes.
Your question about the de Bruijn-Erdos-Turan claim has been addressed on MathOverflow here.

Related to the trivial remark that it is sufficient to understand $f(p)$ and $f(p+1)$, in the sense that $f(p_n+1)\ge f(p_n+2) \ge \ldots \ge f(p_{n+1})$ holds strict, I guess also $$\sum_{p\le x} f(p+1)^2 \sim 4 \pi(x).$$ The latter nevertheless does also not give any indication that the limsup should be infinity.
